perturbation.original_text: 5. “Expiration Date ” means 1 year from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  5. “Expiration Date ” means 90 days from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the expiration date of the NDA to 90 days could be problematic if trade secrets are involved, as trade secret protection can last indefinitely as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage. Reducing the confidentiality period could make it easier for information to lose trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the confidentiality period to 90 days. This contradicts the potential for trade secret protection to last indefinitely under the Economic Espionage Act, provided the information remains confidential and valuable. A shorter NDA period may not adequately protect trade secrets, depending on the nature of the confidential information involved, and could thus be seen as insufficient under the definition of a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined the Expiration Date as one year from the Effective Date, whereas the changed text reduces it to 90 days. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the potential for trade secret protection to last indefinitely under the Economic Espionage Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, it does not contain the specific language regarding indefinite protection of trade secrets. However, the Perturbation and Law Explanations argue that shortening the NDA period to 90 days could be insufficient to protect trade secrets, contradicting the potential for indefinite protection under the Economic Espionage Act. Therefore, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall only have the right to enforce this Agreement by monetary damages, up to a maximum of $1000, and waives any right to injunctive relief, specific performance, or other equitable remedies.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting remedies to monetary damages and waiving injunctive relief can be problematic. Injunctive relief is a common and often necessary remedy for breach of confidentiality, as monetary damages may not fully compensate for the harm caused by the disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Luxembourg law does not have a codified equivalent to the US concept of specific performance, limiting the remedies specifically to monetary damages may unduly restrict the party's rights. Principles of Equity and the court's power to award other remedies can be invoked if monetary damages are insufficient. However, in many situations in Luxembourg specific enforcement is seen as disproportionate, but that does not prohibit the possibility of obtaining such relief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Bill of Rights: A Transcription | National Archives Top Skip to main content The Bill of Rights: A Transcription Note: The following text is a transcription of the enrolled original of the Joint Resolution of Congress proposing the Bill of Rights, which is on permanent display in the Rotunda at the National Archives Museum . The spelling and punctuation reflects the original. On September 25, 1789, the First Congress of the United States proposed 12 amendments to the Constitution. The 1789 Joint Resolution of Congress proposing the amendments is on display in the Rotunda in the National Archives Museum. Ten of the proposed 12 amendments were ratified by three-fourths of the state legislatures on December 15, 1791. The ratified Articles (Articles 3–12) constitute the first 10 amendments of the Constitution, or the U.S. Bill of Rights. In 1992, 203 years after it was proposed, Article 2 was ratified as the 27th Amendment to the Constitution. Article 1 was never ratified. Transcription of the 1789 Joint Resolution of Congress Proposing 12 Amendments to the U.S. Constitution Congress of the United States begun and held at the City of New-York, on Wednesday the fourth of March, one thousand seven hundred and eighty nine. THE Conventions of a number of the States, having at the time of their adopting the Constitution, expressed a desire, in order to prevent misconstruction or abuse of its powers, that further declaratory and restrictive clauses should be added: And as extending the ground of public confidence in the Government, will best ensure the beneficent ends of its institution. RESOLVED by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America, in Congress assembled, two thirds of both Houses concurring, that the following Articles be proposed to the Legislatures of the several States, as amendments to the Constitution of the United States, all, or any of which Articles, when ratified by three fourths of the said Legislatures, to be valid to all intents and purposes, as part of the said Constitution; viz. ARTICLES in addition to, and Amendment of the Constitution of the United States of America, proposed by Congress, and ratified by the Legislatures of the several States, pursuant to the fifth Article of the original Constitution. Article the first... After the first enumeration required by the first article of the Constitution, there shall be one Representative for every thirty thousand, until the number shall amount to one hundred, after which the proportion shall be so regulated by Congress, that there shall be not less than one hundred Representatives, nor less than one Representative for every forty thousand persons, until the number of Representatives shall amount to two hundred; after which the proportion shall be so regulated by Congress, that there shall not be less than two hundred Representatives, nor more than one Representative for every fifty thousand persons. Article the second... No law, varying the compensation for the services of the Senators and Representatives, shall take effect, until an election of Representatives shall have intervened. Article the third... Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the Government for a redress of grievances. Article the fourth... A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed. Article the fifth... No Soldier shall, in time of peace be quartered in any house, without the consent of the Owner, nor in time of war, but in a manner to be prescribed by law. Article the sixth... The right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures, shall not be violated, and no Warrants shall issue, but upon probable cause
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for injunctive relief and specific performance for breaches of confidentiality. The changed contract limits remedies to monetary damages and waives the right to injunctive relief. While the provided law snippets are not directly applicable, the Law Explanation indicates that principles of equity and the court's power to award other remedies (beyond monetary) can be invoked if monetary damages are insufficient in Luxembourg. The Perturbation Explanation further highlights that injunctive relief is a common and necessary remedy for breach of confidentiality. By explicitly waiving injunctive relief and limiting remedies to monetary damages, the changed contract contradicts the possibility of obtaining equitable remedies, which could be deemed necessary based on the specific circumstances of the breach, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiv ing Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall remain in effect for the later of either (i) 5 (five) years after the last date defined in clause 3.2 or (ii) until the Confidential Information may reasonably be considered as no longer confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiv ing Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall terminate immediately upon termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the post-termination confidentiality obligations entirely can be problematic, especially if trade secrets are involved. Trade secret protection can last indefinitely as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage. Eliminating the confidentiality period would make it easier for information to lose trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification eliminates all post-termination obligations for confidentiality. This contradicts the potential for trade secret protection to last indefinitely under the Economic Espionage Act, provided the information remains confidential and valuable.  Terminating the confidentiality agreement simultaneously with the agreement means the confidential information is no longer protectable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification eliminates post-termination confidentiality obligations, potentially contradicting trade secret law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' and indicates that its protection hinges on the owner taking 'reasonable measures to keep such information secret' and the information deriving 'independent economic value' from not being known. By terminating confidentiality obligations immediately upon agreement termination, the changed text removes a key 'reasonable measure' to protect trade secrets, potentially undermining their legal protection. This creates an inconsistency, because the changed agreement fails to account for the indefinite protection afforded to trade secrets if confidentiality is maintained.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of North Korea, and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover. Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing North Korean law as the governing law and venue creates an unenforceable agreement due to lack of access to fair and impartial courts and general principles of public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Choosing North Korean law and courts is highly likely to be deemed unenforceable in most jurisdictions due to the lack of judicial independence, transparency, and fairness in North Korea. This would violate fundamental principles of public policy and render the agreement practically unenforceable. Moreover, the US, for instance, has many sanctions and restrictions that would make commercial enforcement very difficult, if not impossible.

perturbation.justification: The changed text selects North Korean law as the governing law, which creates a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states this creates an unenforceable agreement due to lack of access to fair courts and public policy issues. The Law Explanation supports this, highlighting the lack of judicial independence and fairness in North Korea. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the U.S. Department of State's warning 'Do Not Travel' to North Korea, suggesting significant risks and potentially impacting the enforceability of any contract governed by North Korean law. This creates a strong contradiction, as selecting North Korean law likely renders the agreement unenforceable.
